questions.q: What are the main characteristics of angiosperms, and how do they differ from gymnosperms? (a) Structure of seeds, (b) Floral structure, (c) Growth habit
questions.answer: Angiosperms are the most diverse group of plants and are characterized by the presence of flowers, fruits, and seeds. The main characteristics of angiosperms include: (a) The seeds are produced in a fruit, which is a ripened ovary. The seeds are endospermic, meaning they have a food storage tissue called endosperm. (b) The floral structure of angiosperms consists of four whorls of organs - sepals, petals, stamens, and carpels. The stamens are the male reproductive organs, while the carpels are the female reproductive organs. (c) The growth habit of angiosperms is extremely diverse, ranging from herbs to trees and vines. In contrast, gymnosperms do not produce flowers or fruits and have seeds that are not endospermic. The seeds of gymnosperms are produced in cones, and the growth habit is usually woody.
marking_scheme: Explain the growth habit difference (2 marks)

questions.q: Describe the structure and function of the root system in plants, including the primary and secondary roots. (a) Primary functions, (b) Types of roots, (c) Adaptations for water and nutrient uptake
questions.answer: The root system of a plant consists of primary and secondary roots that play crucial roles in water and nutrient uptake. (a) Primary functions of the root system include: Anchorage - holding the plant in place, Water and nutrient uptake - absorbing water and minerals from the soil, Storage - storing food and water. (b) Types of roots include: Taproot - a single, main root that grows straight down into the soil, Fibrous roots - a mass of fine roots that spread out in all directions, Adventitious roots - roots that grow from other parts of the plant, such as the stem or leaves. (c) Adaptations for water and nutrient uptake include: Root hairs - increase the surface area for absorption, Mycorrhizal association - association with fungi that help in nutrient uptake, Root pressure - helps in upward movement of water.
marking_scheme: Mention the primary functions of the root system (1 mark)
Describe the types of roots (2 marks)
Explain the adaptations for water and nutrient uptake (2 marks)

questions.q: Describe the process of photosynthesis in plants, including the light-dependent and light-independent reactions. (a) Light-dependent reactions, (b) Light-independent reactions, (c) Importance of photosynthesis
questions.answer: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert light energy into chemical energy in the form of glucose. The process of photosynthesis occurs in two stages: (a) Light-dependent reactions - occur in the thylakoid membranes of the chloroplasts and involve the conversion of light energy into ATP and NADPH. This stage includes the absorption of light by pigments such as chlorophyll and the transfer of electrons to electron acceptors. (b) Light-independent reactions - also known as the Calvin cycle, occur in the stroma of the chloroplasts and involve the fixation of CO2 into glucose using the ATP and NADPH produced in the light-dependent reactions. This stage includes the conversion of CO2 into glucose through a series of enzyme-catalyzed reactions. (c) Importance of photosynthesis - it is essential for life on Earth as it provides the energy and organic compounds needed to support life. Without photosynthesis, plants would not be able to grow and thrive, and ultimately, life on Earth would not be possible.
marking_scheme: Discuss the importance of photosynthesis (1 mark)

questions.q: Describe the structure and function of the xylem and phloem tissues in plants. (a) Xylem structure, (b) Phloem structure, (c) Transport of water and nutrients
questions.answer: The xylem and phloem are two types of vascular tissues in plants that play crucial roles in the transport of water and nutrients. (a) Xylem structure - consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibers. The tracheids and vessels are responsible for the transport of water and minerals from the roots to the leaves, while the xylem parenchyma and xylem fibers provide support and protection. (b) Phloem structure - consists of sieve cells, companion cells, phloem parenchyma, and phloem fibers. The sieve cells and companion cells are responsible for the transport of sugars and other organic compounds from the leaves to the rest of the plant, while the phloem parenchyma and phloem fibers provide support and protection. (c) Transport of water and nutrients - the xylem transports water and minerals from the roots to the leaves through a process called transpiration, while the phloem transports sugars and other organic compounds from the leaves to the rest of the plant through a process called translocation.
marking_scheme: Discuss the transport of water and nutrients (1 mark)

questions.q: Describe the process of transpiration in plants, including the factors that affect it. (a) Mechanism of transpiration, (b) Factors affecting transpiration, (c) Importance of transpiration
questions.answer: Transpiration is the process by which plants release water vapor into the air through their leaves. (a) Mechanism of transpiration - occurs through the stomata, which are small openings on the surface of the leaves. The stomata are controlled by guard cells, which open and close in response to changes in light, temperature, and humidity. When the stomata are open, water is absorbed from the soil through the roots and transported to the leaves, where it is evaporated into the air. (b) Factors affecting transpiration - include temperature, humidity, light intensity, and wind speed. As temperature and light intensity increase, transpiration also increases, while high humidity and wind speed can decrease transpiration. (c) Importance of transpiration - it is essential for the growth and development of plants, as it helps to regulate temperature, transport water and minerals, and maintain water balance. Transpiration also plays a crucial role in the water cycle, helping to evaporate water from the surface of the Earth and contributing to the formation of clouds and precipitation.
marking_scheme: Describe the mechanism of transpiration (2 marks)
List the factors affecting transpiration (2 marks)
Discuss the importance of transpiration (1 mark)
